feedback.text: Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act: Overlap with the European Citizens Initiative Stop Destroying Videogames I would like to draw attention to the significant overlap between the proposed Digital Fairness Act and the European Citizens Initiative Stop Destroying Videogames. I request that these considerations be taken into account when drafting and implementing the Act. Currently, games - and increasingly other software - are offered predominantly under a Software as a Service (SaaS) model. This practice denies consumers ownership and provides no guarantee of continued availability. There are several issues with the current approach: Misleading language of sale: Licences are described as purchases, maintaining the outward appearance of traditional ownership despite no transfer of property. The terms of service conceal the true nature of the transaction. (Analogy: car manufacturers sell cars as before, but add a clause saying the buyer does not own the car and the manufacturer may repossess it at any time.) Unreasonable licence revocation: Most licences can be revoked at any time, without reason, making it possible to spend significant sums - both on the base game and in-game purchases - only to have access removed without recourse. This has already happened in practice. Always-online single-player games: Many games with no essential online components are locked behind phone-home checks. When servers are shut down, these games become inaccessible despite being fully functional offline. (Analogy: your car will not start unless it calls a specific number, and that number is later disconnected.) Suppression of preservation efforts: Communities that attempt to restore unsupported games are often threatened with legal action, even when the publisher has abandoned support. (Analogy: a car manufacturer closes all service centers but threatens to sue if owners repair their own cars.) The initiative calls for minimal but important regulation: End-of-life plans for all games sold as services, ensuring they remain usable offline after official support ends. Legal protection for non-profit preservation, akin to the allowances made for books and other media in libraries and museums, so that volunteers can restore abandoned games to a playable state without risk of legal action. This could be implemented through two provisions: A requirement that the final update to a discontinued game removes phone-home functionality. A fair-use exemption allowing non-profit groups to maintain or restore discontinued titles.
